title: Market equilibrium and disequilibrium
description: Practice what you have learned about how a market generates an equilibrium price and quantity, and the cause of surpluses and shortages in this exercise.
topic_path: Market equilibrium, disequilibrium, and changes in equilibrium

assessment_items.question_content:
*The market for blankets is shown here.*

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following explains why $\$7$ is not an equilibrium price in the market for blankets?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

options.alt: A coordinate grid plots two labeled straight lines and three points. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and is marked from 0 to 11 in increments of 1. The vertical axis is labeled P and is marked from 0 to 17 in increments of 1. One upward-sloping line from bottom left to top right is labeled S. One downward-sloping line from top left to bottom right is labeled D. Line S joins (0, 1) and (7, 15). Line D joins (0, 13) and (10, 3). Three points are plotted along the horizontal line at the coordinates (3, 7), (6, 7), and (0, 7).

choices.content: The quantity demanded and quantity supplied are equal
The supply is greater than the demand
The quantity demanded is greater than the quantity supplied
The quantity supplied is greater than the quantity demanded
The demand is greater than the supply

assessment_items.question_content:
*The market for hamster food is shown here.*

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following represents the shortage that would result in this market at a price of $P_5$?**

[[☃ radio 3]]

options.alt: A coordinate plane plots two labeled straight lines and six reference points. The horizontal axis is labeled Q, and the vertical axis is labeled P. The axes are unmarked. Line S is upward-sloping from bottom left to top right. Line D is downward-sloping from top left to bottom right. The two lines intersect at point X. Three dashed horizontal lines extend from the vertical axis and are labeled P subscript 3, P subscript 4, and P subscript 5, with P subscript 3 at the highest level and P subscript 5 at the lowest. Three dashed vertical lines rise from the horizontal axis and are labeled Q subscript A, Q subscript B, and Q subscript C. Point X lies at the intersection of Q subscript B and P subscript 4. Additional intersections are marked where the dashed lines meet the supply and demand curves at all three levels.

assessment_items.question_content:
*This graph represents the market for time machines*.*

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the equilibrium price and quantity in this market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Please note: "bagillion" is an imaginary value, just as time machines are an imaginary invention*

options.alt: A coordinate grid plots two labeled straight lines. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and is marked from 0 to 5 in increments of 1. The vertical axis is labeled P (bagillion dollars) and is marked from 0 to 16 in increments of 1. One upward-sloping line from (0, 8) to (4, 16) is labeled S. One downward-sloping line from (0, 6) to (4, 2) is labeled D.

choices.content: There is no equilibrium in this market.
The equilibrium will be at a negative price and negative quantity.

assessment_items.question_content:
*The demand and supply schedules for lawn mowing are given below:*

Price | Quantity demanded | Quantity Supplied
-|-
$\$20$ | $0$| $12$
$\$18$ | $2$| $10$
$\$16$ | $2$| $8$ 
$\$14$ | $3$ | $6$
$\$12$ | $4$ | $4$
$\$10$ | $5$ | $2$
$\$8$ | $6$ | $0$

**Which of the following best describes why a price of $\$16$ would not be considered an equilibrium price?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Quantity supplied is less than quantity demanded, causing a surplus, which will cause the price to fall
Quantity supplied is greater than quantity demanded, causing a shortage, which will cause the price to rise.
Quantity supplied is less than quantity demanded, causing a shortage, which will cause the price to rise.
Quantity supplied is greater than quantity demanded, causing a surplus, which will cause the price to fall.
Quantity supplied is greater than quantity demanded, causing a surplus, which will cause the price to rise.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes a market in equilibrium?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: At the current price, quantity supplied equals quantity demanded.
At the current price, quantity supplied is greater than quantity demanded.
At the current price, quantity supplied is less than quantity demanded.
At the current quantity, buyers are willing to pay more than sellers receive.
At the current quantity, the price sellers charge is more than what buyers are willing to pay.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following terms describes a situation in which there is an excess quantity demanded in a market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which of the following terms describes a situation in which there is an excess quantity supplied in a market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which of the following best describes the sequences of events that lead to a price increase and quantity decrease when supply decreases?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: When supply decreases, there is a shortage because $Q_S<Q_D$ at the old price. Price will adjust upward until the market clears at a new lower quantity.
When supply decreases, there is a surplus because $Q_S<Q_D$ at the old price. Price will adjust upward until the market clears at a new lower quantity.
When supply decreases, there is a shortage because $Q_S>Q_D$ at the old price. Price will adjust upward until the market clears at a new lower quantity.
When supply decreases, there is a surplus because $Q_S>Q_D$ at the old price. Price will adjust upward until the market clears at a new lower quantity.
When supply decreases, the price increases automatically to make $Q_S=Q_D$.
